lede: Poetry & Literature · วรรณกรรม accounts for 112 catalogued manuscripts. It clusters in Phayao (18% of the corpus for this genre), ahead of Nan and Chiang Mai. Nearly all (85%) are written in Tham Lanna · อักษรธรรมล้านนา script. By support it leans to palm-leaf · ใบลาน (65%) over mulberry paper (saa) · กระดาษสา (21%). Dated witnesses run 1836–1989 CE (54 of 112 carry a date).

authored.title: Poetry & Literature
authored.body_html:
What this is

This is the collection's belletristic layer — literature for pleasure, the closest this archive comes to storytelling for its own sake. The catalogue splits it as Folk Tale (63%) and Secular Literary Work (37%), and the titles bear both out.

On one side, the folk romances and tales: Khun Sam Lo, the great northern love-tragedy; Cao saeng moeng; Suwattang khanthang; the nithan of the Himavanta forest (Tamnan nitan pa mai himapan) with its mythical beasts. On the other, the sung-verse forms that are the native vehicle of Lanna literature — the khao and the kham khap (Kam khap, Kam khap ka long, Khao doi …), poetry meant to be performed aloud, not silently read. The corpus is also porous in a way the learned genres are not: a khao retelling of Vessantara (Kam len khao wetsantala) turns scripture into entertainment, and a copy of Phra Aphai Mani shows Central Thai literature travelling north into Lanna hands.

For the wider project this is the living vernacular voice — the register where the tradition entertained itself, where oral performance met the page, and the one that connects most directly to still-living Lanna storytelling and its documented folklore.

The shape of the collection

Two features set this genre apart from the learned corpora. First, it is the latest- dating genre in the collection: no witness is older than 1836, and the bulk is 20th century — vernacular literature was written down only recently, long after the scripture and the grammars. Second, its support and geography break the canonical mould — paper runs to 21% (against the 3–9% of the scholastic genres), and it is not a Wat Sung Men tradition at all: Phayao and Nan lead, Phrae trails, and Tai Lue temples like Wat Nong Bua figure. The folk literature lived away from the great scholastic library.

Notes

  • The most exposed genre, and the most urgent. Because these forms are oral and performed, and were committed to writing only from the 19th century, this is the corpus most under-represented on the shelf — its life is in performance, not the page. Because the manuscript record is thin here, the khao and kham khap material deserves early attention despite its small catalogue footprint — much of it lives in the oral and contemporary record rather than on palm-leaf.
  • Porous boundaries — it absorbs from everywhere. Central Thai imports (Phra Aphai Mani), jātaka retold as entertainment (the khao Vessantara), and folk tales that shade into chronicle all sit here. Expect overlap with jātaka wherever a birth-story is told for pleasure rather than merit.
  • Border diversity. A real Tai Lue and Shan presence, higher than the learned genres', marks where secular literature crossed ethnic and linguistic lines — folk story travels more freely than canon.
  • One label, two objects. Oral folk tale and written secular literary work are distinct traditions collapsed together; separating them is the obvious next pass.
